rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff Scott J. Mandell. Per Ex Parte Order Filed On 7-3-19.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, July 25, 2019, line 12. Defendant's Motion To Compel Deposition Of Plaintiff Scott J. Mandell. Defendants and Cross-Complainants Guerneville Business Corporation and Ray K. Shahani's motion to compel Cross-Complainant Scott J. Mandell to appear for an oral deposition is granted on the following conditions. Mr. Mandell may be deposed by Skype or videoconference for a total of 3 hours. In view of the upcoming August 5, 2019 trial date, the deposition shall take place no later than July 31, 2019. The deposition shall be transcribed by a certified court reporter present at Mr. Mandell's location. The deposing parties shall provide to Mr. Mandell and counsel in advance of the deposition premarked exhibits they intend to use during the deposition. Both parties' requests for sanctions are denied. In addition, counsel shall appear (in person or telephonically) to address whether this case should be referred to the Court's Judicial Mediation Program. (See description in "Judicial Mediation Program Information Sheet," available on the Court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/civil/dispute-resolution.) rulings.Case Title: EVA ARMADA ET AL VS. MICHAEL GUMBAN PANGILINAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Terminating And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, July 25, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFFS EVA ARMADA, ANTONIO SALSEDO, RUDY CONCEPCION, AND LORNA BINALEY'S Motion For Terminating And Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Specially set at 1:30 p.m. before Judge Pro Tem David McDonald. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to davidmcdonald@prodigy.net with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NICHOLAS RENAULT VS. DANTE DALPORTO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Oppose Good Faith Settlement Determination
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, July 25, 2019, line 4. DEFENDANT DANTE DALPORTO'S Motion To Oppose Good Faith Settlement Determination. Defendant Dante Dalporto's motion contesting Defendants Molly Alsnauer and Erick Perez's application for good faith settlement determination is denied, and Defendants' application for a good faith settlement determination is granted. Mr. Perez has agreed to pay $15,000 to Plaintiff Nicholas Renault, the entire amount of his available liability insurance coverage, and his declaration shows that he has exceedingly modest assets and is effectively judgment proof. (Perez Decl. 3-6; see Dole Food Co., Inc. v. Superior Court (2015) 242 Cal.App.4th 894, 909 [relevant considerations include the financial conditions and insurance policy limits of settling defendants]; County of Los Angeles v. Guerrero (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1149, 1152 ["Notwithstanding the settlement's disproportionality, because Guerrero lacks other assets, substantial evidence supports the finding of good faith settlement"].) Under the circumstances, Mr. Dalporto fails to meet his burden to show that the settlement is not in good faith. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 877.6(d) [the party asserting the lack of good faith shall have the burden of proof on that issue]; Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Assocs. (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.) Mr. Dalporto's contention that the motion is "premature" because discovery has just commenced lacks merit. The litigation has been pending for over one year, since June 2018. In any event, a good faith determination may be sought even before litigation is filed. (County of Los Angeles v. Guerrero, 209 Cal.App.3d at 1155 ["the fact that the instant settlement was entered into early does not preclude a finding of good faith"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MARILYN A. BARTHOLOMEW VS. GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff'S First Amended Complaint (Filed May-20-2019)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, July 25, 2019, line 9. Defendants' Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiff's First Amended Complaint. Defendants Golden Gate Tow Inc., The John Stewart Company, and Daniel Ingram Stone dba Cornerstone Real Estate Services, LLC's motion to strike portions of Plaintiff Marilyn Bartholomew's first amended complaint is granted in part with 30 days leave to amend. Although the parties continue to contest whether they have met and conferred as required, rather than further delay a ruling on the sufficiency of the operative pleading, the Court sees fit to issue a decision on the merits based on the briefing previously submitted. Although Plaintiff states that she intends to amend her complaint, she did not move for leave to do so. Instead, the Court will liberally allow amendments to her complaint to cure allegations subject to this motion to strike. (See Vaccaro v. Kaiman (1998) 63 Cal.App.4th 761, 768 ["When the defect which justifies striking a complaint is capable of cure, the court should allow leave to amend."].) The allegations at paragraphs 57, 60, 63, and 67 pertaining to calculations of damages for violations of Vehicle Code section 22658 are improper and are stricken. The plain language of the statute suggests that damages are calculated based on charges actually assessed, not based on hypothetical calculations. (Veh. Code §§ 22658(e)(1), (l)(5), (m)(3).) Plaintiff has leave to amend these allegations if she can allege in good faith that she was subjected to towing and storage charges under the relevant statutory provisions. Plaintiff should not allege hypothetical amounts of damages, but money she was actually required to pay. The attorneys' fees request in the prayer for relief is improper because there is no allegation that forms a basis for it in contract or statute. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 1021 ["Except as attorney's fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties"].) Plaintiff misapplies the tort-of-another doctrine. Beraksa v. Stardust Records, Inc. (1963) 215 Cal.App.2d 708 relied upon Civil Code section 3318 involving breach of warranty of an agent's authority which provides for "reasonable expenses of legal proceedings taken, in good faith, to enforce the act of the agent against his principal." Moreover, self-represented litigants usually cannot recover attorneys' fees. (See Trope v. Katz (1995) 11 Cal.4th 274, 285, 292 [holding that pro se litigants cannot recover reasonable attorney's fees under Civil Code section 1717].) Plaintiff has leave to amend if she can allege in good faith that there is a contractual or statutory basis for attorneys' fees. The motion to strike paragraphs 6, 13, 15, 33, 45, and 68 of the complaint is denied. Those allegations contain potentially relevant background information, and are not "irrelevant, false, or improper matter" subject to being stricken under Code of Civil Procedure Section 436. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MARILYN A. BARTHOLOMEW VS. GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Golden Gate Tow, Inc., The John Stewart Company, Daniel Stone Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (Added For Purposes Of Entering Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Defendants' Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint *** Tentative Ruling - Part 2 of 2 *** Plaintiff's fifth cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress (IIED) fails because Plaintiff has not alleged conduct that is extreme and outrageous as a matter of law. The trial court may determine whether a defendant's conduct may reasonably be regarded as so extreme and outrageous as to permit recovery for IIED. (Plotnik v. Meihaus (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1590, 1614.) Without allegations of conduct that goes far beyond the annoyance and inconvenience of deceptive car towing, the FAC does not form the basis for an IIED claim. (See Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209 [outrageous conduct must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community"]; Yurick v. Superior Court (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1116, 1128 ["ordinary defendants are not liable for mere insult, indignity, annoyance, or even threats, where the case is lacking other circumstances of aggravation."].) Moreover, the FAC alleges that Plaintiff was leasing her vehicle to another individual and that she was not present when the towing occurred. (FAC 13, 15.) Therefore, Plaintiff was not deprived of a means of transportation. (See Christensen v. Superior Court (1991) 54 Cal.3d 868, 903-904 [an IIED claim requires conduct directed at the plaintiff or conduct that occurs in presence of plaintiff of whom defendant is aware].) With regard to the sixth cause of action for promissory estoppel, Plaintiff has alleged a clear and unambiguous promise, but must clarify the nature and scope of her reliance to state a cause of action. (FAC 28, 30, 113-114; see Thomson v. International Alliance of Stage Employees (1965) 232 Cal.App.2d 446, 454 [promissory estoppel claim requires a clear and unambiguous promise and reasonable and foreseeable reliance that causes injury].) While it is reasonable and foreseeable that Plaintiff would rely on the alleged promise that she could pick up her car from Defendants free of charge, the extent of Plaintiff's reasonable reliance and injury from her lost opportunity to "avoid being injured by Defendants' secret scheme" is unclear as currently alleged. (FAC 114.) Plaintiff has leave to amend to clarify these allegations. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. *** End of Tentative Ruling *** =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MARILYN A. BARTHOLOMEW VS. GOLDEN GATE TOW, INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, July 25, 2019, line 9. *** Part 1 of 2 *** DEFENDANTS' DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Golden Gate Tow Inc., The John Stewart Company, and Daniel Ingram Stone dba Cornerstone Real Estate Services, LLC's demurrer to Plaintiff Marilyn Bartholomew's first amended complaint (FAC) is sustained as to each of the five causes of action with 30 days leave to amend. Although the parties continue to contest whether they have met and conferred as required, rather than further delay a ruling on the sufficiency of the operative pleading, the Court sees fit to issue a decision on the merits based on the briefing previously submitted. Although Plaintiff states that she intends to amend her complaint, she did not move for leave to do so. Regardless, the Court, in sustaining Defendants' demurrer, will adhere to the liberal policy of allowing amendments to cure defects. (See Aubry v. Tri-City Hospital Dist. (1992) 2 Cal.4th 962, 970-971 ["Where the complaint is defective, in the furtherance of justice great liberality should be exercised in permitting a plaintiff to amend his complaint, and it ordinarily constitutes an abuse of discretion to sustain a demurrer without leave to amend if there is a reasonable possibility that the defect can be cured by amendment."] [internal quotations omitted].) Plaintiff's first cause of action for fraud fails because it relies upon unclear and conclusory allegations of "justifiable reliance." (FAC 78; see Wilhelm v. Pray Price, Williams & Russell (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1324, 1331 [fraud claims require "justifiable reliance"]; Hall v. Department of Adoptions (1975) 47 Cal.App.3d 898, 904 [fraud claims must be pled with specificity].) Plaintiff has leave to amend her complaint to clarify how she justifiably relied upon Defendants' alleged statements, especially considering that she questioned Defendants' basis for towing the vehicle based on contrary evidence. (FAC 17, 19, 27.) In the absence of a properly stated fraud claim, Plaintiff also fails to state a prima facie case for the second cause of action for conspiracy to defraud. (See Okun v. Superior Court (1981) 29 Cal.3d 442, 454 ["Though conspiracy may render additional parties liable for the wrong, the conspiracy itself is not actionable without a wrong."].) The third cause of action for violations of Vehicle Code section 22658 fails because Plaintiff's FAC omits specific allegations that Plaintiff was ever assessed any towing or storage charges for the January 10, 2018 tow or the period of storage from January 10, 2018 to May 2, 2018. A plain reading of all three of the Vehicle Code provisions referenced in the FAC indicates that liability stems from towing and storage charges actually assessed to an individual. (Veh. Code §§ 22658(e)(1), (l)(5), (m)(3).) Even considering the allegations that the amounts for towing and storage were concealed, this does not provide a basis for Plaintiff to perform calculations of hypothetical charges in the complaint. (FAC 53, 57, 60, 63, 67.) Plaintiff has leave to amend if she can allege in good faith that she was subjected to towing and storage charges under the relevant statutory provisions. *** End of Part 1 - Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2 ***
